request: Visual: A number line from 0 to 10 with every whole number labeled and evenly spaced tick marks. Purpose: Kindergarten students use the number line to count forward and locate whole numbers.
Visual: A circle divided into 2 equal parts with 1 part shaded. Purpose: Students practice naming the fraction (one-half) for EML language development.
Visual: A rectangle divided into 3 equal parts with 2 parts shaded, representing 2/3 of a whole. Purpose: Students identify the starting fraction before beginning division problems.

Visual: A bar graph titled 'Animals at the Zoo' with a horizontal axis labeled from 0 to 10 with tick marks at each number, and two vertical bars representing Lions (height 9) and Tigers (height 6). Each bar is a different color and labeled at the base. Purpose: A reference example showing students how a completed bar graph displays animal count data, supporting their work in Lesson 13 using bar graphs to compare.

Visual: A parallelogram with base 8 units and height 5 units labeled, with a diagonal line drawn from one corner to the opposite corner dividing it into two triangles. Purpose: Students understand how a parallelogram can be split into two equal triangles to visualize the triangle area formula.
Visual: A ten-frame (a 2-by-5 grid of squares) with 7 of the cells filled with counters and 3 left empty. Purpose: Kindergarten students see how 7 is composed within ten and how many more make ten.

Visual: A number line from 0 to 2, with three jumps marked: first jump to 1/4, second jump to 1/2 (landing at 3/4), and third jump to 1/4 (landing at 1). Purpose: Students identify which addition equation matches this visual representation of combining fourths.

Visual: A smooth opaque rectangular prism (no unit cubes) with visible edge labels: 5 units along one edge, 3 units along another edge, and 2 units along the height. Purpose: Students see a prism with clear dimensions to apply the volume formula V = length × width × height.

Visual: A set model showing 12 circles arranged in a 3-by-4 array, with 9 circles shaded in one color and 3 circles unshaded, representing 3/4 of the set. Purpose: Students use a set model to understand fractions as parts of a group before division.

Visual: A blank analog clock face with numbers 1-12 labeled and minute marks around the perimeter, but no hands drawn. Purpose: Grade 1 students draw hour and minute hands to show times for their Sunday schedule (multiple blank clocks needed for practice).
Visual: A horizontal number line from -10 to 10 with tick marks at every integer and labels every 5 units. Purpose: Students locate positive and negative integers and see zero as the midpoint.

Visual: A line plot showing the number of hours students spent playing sports in one week, with x-axis labeled 'Hours' (0 to 7 hours in increments of 1), and dots placed to represent: 1 student at 2 hours, 2 students at 3 hours, 3 students at 4 hours, 2 students at 5 hours, 1 student at 6 hours. Purpose: Students interpret data from a completed line plot to answer questions about the most common number of hours, how many students played sports, and comparing different hours.

Visual: A coordinate grid from 0 to 5 on the x-axis and 0 to 12 on the y-axis, with gridlines showing increments of 1, no points plotted. Purpose: Students plot the ordered pairs (1,5), (2,7), (3,9), (4,11) to determine if the relationship is proportional, then compare with a proportional line.

Visual: Two rectangular prisms shown separately and clearly distinguished by color or shading. First prism on the left, second prism on the right. No specific dimensions are labeled on the prisms themselves. Purpose: Students use this visual to understand that a composite figure made of two prisms can be decomposed, and they can add the volumes of the two separate prisms together.
Visual: A circle divided into 8 equal parts with 5 parts shaded. Purpose: Students name the shaded fraction five-eighths and compare it to one half.

Visual: A horizontal rectangular strip labeled 6 feet long, divided into 4 equal sections with each section labeled 1/4 foot, with no answer shown and no count of pieces given. Purpose: Students practice dividing a whole number by a unit fraction by counting or reasoning about how many 1/4-foot pieces fit in a 6-foot strip.

Visual: A bar graph titled 'Favorite Sports' with a horizontal axis labeled from 0 to 14 with tick marks at each number, and three vertical bars representing Soccer (height 12), Basketball (height 8), and Baseball (height 5). Each bar is a different color and labeled at the base. Purpose: A reference example showing students how a completed bar graph displays sports preference data, supporting their work in Lesson 9 interpreting bar graphs.

Visual: A number line from 0 to 10 showing the inequality x > 3: an open circle at 3 with the line shaded to the right and an arrow continuing past 10. Purpose: Students represent the solution set of a one-variable inequality, paying attention to the open circle meaning 3 is not included.

Visual: A number line from 0 to 1 with marks at 0, 1/6, 2/6, 3/6, 4/6, 5/6, and 1. The distance from 0 to 2/3 is highlighted, with 4 brackets showing how many 1/6 sections fit inside 2/3. Purpose: Students use a linear model to count how many 1/6 units fit into 2/3.

Visual: Two equal-length fraction bars stacked vertically and aligned at the left. The top bar is divided into 2 equal parts with 1 shaded; the bottom bar is divided into 4 equal parts with 2 shaded. Purpose: Students see that 1/2 and 2/4 cover the same length, building the idea of equivalent fractions.

Visual: A clock with the short hand pointing to 6 and the long hand pointing to 12. Purpose: Grade 1 students analyze a confusing clock where hands could be mistaken - learning to identify that the short hand points to 6 (meaning 6 o'clock) and the long hand points to 12 (on the hour).

Visual: An area model for 4 × 6: a rectangle partitioned into a 4-by-6 grid of equal unit squares, with the rows and columns countable. Purpose: Students connect repeated addition to multiplication by counting rows and columns of equal squares.
Visual: A circle divided into 4 equal parts with 3 parts shaded. Purpose: Students identify and name the shaded fraction (three-fourths) for language practice.

Visual: Base-ten blocks representing the number 234: 2 hundred-flats (10-by-10 squares), 3 ten-rods (1-by-10 strips), and 4 unit-cubes, grouped and clearly separated. Purpose: Students connect the place-value meaning of 234 to a concrete model of hundreds, tens, and ones.

Visual: A horizontal rectangular strip labeled 5 feet long, divided into 3 equal sections with each section labeled 1/3 foot, with no answer shown and no count of pieces given. Purpose: Students practice dividing a whole number by a unit fraction by counting or reasoning about how many 1/3-foot pieces fit in a 5-foot strip.

Visual: A coordinate plane with x- and y-axes from -5 to 5, gridlines at every integer, with the single point (-3, 2) plotted and labeled. Purpose: Students practice locating an ordered pair in the second quadrant using signed coordinates.

Visual: Dot groups arranged in rows and columns representing addition, with colorful dots in two different colors (like a rainbow gradient), suitable for visualizing addition. Purpose: A decorative image for 'Addition Array Battle' game where students create groups of objects.

Visual: An analog clock showing 3:15, with the hour hand just past 3 and the minute hand pointing at 3 (the 15-minute mark). Purpose: Students read time to the quarter hour and notice the hour hand moves as the minutes pass.

Visual: A bar graph titled 'Favorite Fruits' with a vertical axis from 0 to 10 and four bars: Apples (7), Bananas (9), Grapes (4), and Oranges (6). Each bar is a different color and labeled at the base. Purpose: Students read and compare values from a completed bar graph with several categories.

Visual: A right triangle with the two legs labeled 6 cm and 8 cm and a small square marking the right angle between them. The hypotenuse is left unlabeled. Purpose: Students apply the area formula or the Pythagorean theorem; the hypotenuse is left blank so the answer isn't given away.
